scenario: i hope i did n't hurt it again
patient_utterances: i hope i did n't hurt it again
[Clinician asked: "hi gregory how are you i the medical assistant told me that you injured your knee"] yeah yeah i heard it stepping off a curb
[Clinician asked: "how long ago did that happen"] about two and a half weeks ago
[Clinician asked: "two and a half weeks ago and what what knee is bothering you"] the right knee the outside of my right knee
[Clinician asked: "the outside of your right knee"] yeah
[Clinician asked: "okay and did you actually fall down when this happened or"] no i just sorta turned over my ankle
[Clinician asked: "okay"] to the outside and i thought my ankle was the thing that was gon na hurt most but after the ankle soreness went away the only my knee hurt and it sort of clicks when i walk
[Clinician asked: "and no okay so now it clicks when you walk okay does it hurt when you walk"] no unless i stand on one foot for an extended period of time like leaning my foot up against the wall
[Clinician asked: "okay"] then i feel like it's locked and then i sort of have to ease into walking again
[Clinician asked: "okay and what have you taken for the pain"] really nothing
[Clinician asked: "okay"] ibuprofen but i'm not a big pill taker so i sort of sucked it up and

[Clinician asked: "does the ibuprofen work"] at night you know during the day i'm more active so it it really does n't impact it because i just work through it but at night it does help me to settle down and sleep better

[Clinician asked: "does your knee hurt when you're just resting and sitting there"] no when it's up it's fine when it's down on the floor with my foot flat it's fine it does hurt when i rest it on my heel

[Clinician asked: "okay and are you able to do things like bending or things like that"] a full squat that's when i feel the most clicking like if i go all the way down to a full squat like a catcher that's when i feel the most clicking but otherwise no there is no pain it's just the clicking and i'm fearful that that's an injury of some type
[Clinician asked: "okay alright now you said squats are you are you active are you doing like burpees going to"] no i ride the bike
[Clinician asked: "okay"] i ride the bike i ride the bike which i'm not doing now peloton inside for the winter i do have a winter bike though
[Clinician asked: "okay alright well i have a peloton who is your favorite instructor"] i i actually do n't have a favorite instructor i have more favorite rides
[Clinician asked: "okay what kind of rides do you like"] i like rock music and regain music rides so whoever is doing those in a began or a medium content i'm cool with

[Clinician asked: "i ca n't picture you as a regade person so that's interesting to know okay alright and any numbing or tingling in your foot at all"] yes but not from the knee injury i broke my foot my second toe on my right foot and i have numbness depending upon the shoe i wear
[Clinician asked: "okay when did you break your toe"] i've broken it three times over the last ten years so it's just something that's a an annoyance but it was n't caused by this injury
[Clinician asked: "okay so you do have some numbing and tingling but it's not new"] right correct

[Clinician asked: "alright alright well let's just go ahead and i'm gon na go ahead and do a quick physical exam i'm gon na be calling out my exam findings i'll let you know what that means so on your musculoskeletal exam on your right knee i do appreciate some slight edema you do have a slight effusion does it hurt w"] no not physically
[Clinician asked: "okay there is no pain to palpation of the right lateral knee i'm gon na be bending your knee in all sorts of positions does that hurt"] only when you pull it to the outside
[Clinician asked: "okay"] from my my foot when you pull it to the outside that's the only time it hurts

[Clinician asked: "okay the patient has a positive varus test there is full range of motion there is a negative lachman sign the patient does on cardiovascular exam the patient does have a palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibialis pulse okay well let's just i wan na talk a little bit about i had the nurse do an x"] yeah i actually i'm at home so i can ice it and i have been icing it
[Clinician asked: "okay"] i mean twenty on twenty off at least four times a day

[Clinician asked: "okay and so does that help at all"] it's it it makes it feel better in the morning when i first go to my desk and at the end of the day before bed you know i take ibuprofen so i do n't have swelling overnight but honestly i have a pretty decent threshold for pain so it's not good it's not changing it by much

[Clinician asked: "okay do you feel like your symptoms are getting worse or better i think i think pretty much for the last two weeks they've remained about the same the the only time i feel like they get worse is when i exert myself doing up and down like gardening in the yard or moving pots"] and stuff outside that's the only time i felt like it got any worse but nothing to the point where it stayed worse
[Clinician asked: "okay alright well let's go ahead and refer you to physical therapy if you're still having pain then we might have to consider an mri"] okay
[Clinician asked: "any questions about that"] no
[Clinician asked: "okay alright well it was very nice seeing you today call me if you need anything"] i will
[Clinician asked: "okay bye"] bye
gold_note:
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Gregory Price is a pleasant 68-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of a right knee injury. The onset of his pain began 2.5 weeks ago, when he stepped off of a curb and turned over his ankle. He locates his pain to the lateral aspect of his right knee. The patient also reports clicking with ambulation. He denies any pain with ambulation unless he stands on one foot for an extended period of time. The patient also reports locking of his knee with prolonged standing. He notices pain when he is resting his foot on his heel. He denies any pain at rest and elevated, or when his foot is flat on the ground. The patient states that he is more active during the day, which does not impact his knee pain. He adds that he is able to perform a full squat without pain. The patient reports numbness and tingling in his right foot, however this is not new. The patient has been taking ibuprofen, which provides him with relief at night. He has also been icing his knee 4 times per day, which provides him with relief in the morning and at the end of the day.

MEDICAL HISTORY

He states that he has fractured his 2nd toe 3 times over the last 10 years.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient works in sales. He rides a bike for exercise.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Neurological: Positive for numbness and tingling in his toes on his right foot from prior injury.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: >Palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibialis pulse.
MSK: Examination of the right knee: Slight edema. Slight effusion. No pain with palpation of the right lateral knee. Positive varus test. Full ROM. Negative Lachman's.

RESULTS

4 views of the right knee were taken. These reveal no evidence of any fractures, dislocations, or bony abnormality. No other abnormalities are noted.

ASSESSMENT

Right knee lateral collateral ligament strain.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays did not reveal any acute fractures or bony abnormality. I have recommended that we treat the patient conservatively with formal physical therapy to strengthen his right knee. I have also prescribed the patient meloxicam 15 mg to treat his pain. If his pain does not improve with physical therapy, I will recommend obtaining an MRI.

naive.response_text:
Gregory, I understand how frustrating it must be to deal with this knee issue for over two weeks now. It’s important to take things step by step, and we’ve decided that starting with physical therapy makes sense. This will help us address the clicking and any underlying issues. In the meantime, continue with the icing and ibuprofen as you’ve been doing. They seem to provide some relief, so keep that up.

Please let me know if you have any questions or if there’s anything else on your mind. We’ll keep a close eye on your progress and adjust the plan as needed. You’re not alone in this, so feel free to reach out if you need anything.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing all the details about your knee injury, Gregory. It makes sense that you're feeling concerned, especially with the persistent clicking and discomfort. Dealing with an injury that doesn't seem to heal as expected can be frustrating, and I appreciate your efforts in managing it with ice and ibuprofen despite not being a big pill taker.

From what you've described, the clicking and locking in your knee, along with the tenderness on the outside, suggest we need to explore this further. While the physical exam didn't show any signs of a serious injury like a ligament tear, the ongoing symptoms indicate that it's worth looking into further. I can understand your fear about this being something more serious, and I want to make sure we address it properly.

We'll start with a referral to physical therapy to help strengthen the muscles around your knee and improve its mobility. In the meantime, continue with the icing and ibuprofen as you've been doing. If the physical therapy doesn't help, we can consider an MRI to get a clearer picture of what's going on. Before you leave, could you summarize what we've discussed so far? That way, I can ensure you're clear on the plan. We'll follow up in a couple of weeks to see how things are progressing.

full.response_text:
It sounds like you're dealing with a few different issues with your knee, foot, and toe. Let me break this down and make sure I understand what's going on.

For a knee injury that happened about two and a half weeks ago, it's important to consider how to manage the pain and improve mobility. Based on clinical guidelines, exercises can help prevent further issues and promote healing.

Regarding the recent foot injury with numbness, we should explore possible causes and ensure proper healing. Guidelines suggest focusing on rest and monitoring symptoms closely.

The toe injury, which has been an annoyance over the years, should be evaluated to rule out any long-term issues. Management typically involves monitoring and addressing symptoms as they arise.
